task: I’m trying to get a small property sale back on track after the title company kicked back a few items and the county wants a revised filing. Can you help me figure out the cleanest order to handle the paperwork so I don’t lose time or create another round of corrections?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the cleanest way is to work **top-down from authority and recordability**, then only circulate the corrected package once the defects are cured.

## Best order to handle it

### 1) Identify the exact defects first
Get the title company’s **full rejection list** and the county’s **revision comments** in writing.

You want:
- what was rejected
- why it was rejected
- whether it is a **title issue**, **execution issue**, or **recording format issue**
- whether the county wants a **new filing** or just a **corrected page / affidavit / cover sheet**

If you don’t separate those, people start “fixing” the wrong thing.

---

### 2) Confirm who has authority to sign
Before re-circulating anything, verify:
- who is authorized to sign for seller
- who is authorized to sign for buyer
- whether any entity docs, resolutions, POAs, trusts, or officer authority letters are needed
- whether the names on the signatures match the names on title and the entity docs

If authority is unclear, stop there and cure it first. A clean form signed by the wrong person still gets kicked.

---

### 3) Fix the county filing issue next
If the county wants a revised filing, handle that before title re-review.

Typical items:
- legal description
- vesting language
- parcel ID / tax ID
- grantor / grantee names
- acknowledgment / notarization format
- margins, font, page size, cover sheet, transfer form, recording fee

Make sure the revised version is **recordable as submitted**, not “almost right.”

---

### 4) Cure title objections in parallel
While the county revision is being prepared, resolve the title issues:
- payoff statements
- lien releases
- judgment releases
- HOA/POA estoppels
- UCC terminations
- deed corrections
- entity name mismatches
- missing probate / authority docs

If title kicked it back, ask for the **minimum cure package** they will accept for re-issue.

---

### 5) Rebuild the closing package as a single clean set
Once the corrected filing and title cures are ready, assemble one final package:
- corrected deed / transfer document
- revised county filing
- execution pages
- notary acknowledgments
- authority docs
- title company requirements
- payoff and release documents

Do not drip-feed pieces if you can avoid it. That usually creates a second review cycle.

---

### 6) Re-submit in the right sequence
A practical sequence is:
1. confirm authority
2. finalize corrected county filing
3. get title to confirm cure sufficiency
4. submit the full package together
5. request one consolidated review/recording

If the county must record first to clear title, confirm that with the title officer before sending anything.

---

## Practical rule
If a document affects **signing authority, vesting, or recordability**, cure it first.  
If it is only a **supporting payoff / release / backup item**, cure it in parallel but don’t let it delay the final package unless title says it’s required.

---

## What I’d want in hand before re-submitting
- title rejection list
- county revision comments
- final legal description
- entity authority docs
- corrected deed or transfer instrument
- payoff/release stack
- title officer’s written “OK to proceed”
- county-ready recording package

---

## Fastest way to avoid another round
Send one email to everyone involved with:
- the defect list
- the corrected draft
- what is still outstanding
- who is responsible for each item
- the exact date/time you want final sign-off

That forces everyone to react to the same version.

If you want, I can turn this into a **hard close-out checklist** with columns for **item / seller support / buyer verification / condition-to-close flag** so you can use it with the broker, title officer, and county.

user_simulator.text:
Close enough, but I want this tighter and more operational.

You gave the right sequence, but I need the **decision tree** version — because not every kickback gets handled the same way. I don’t want a generic checklist, I want:

1) **what gets cured first if the county defect is recordability vs. content vs. indexing**
2) **what title will actually wait on** versus what they’ll clear conditionally
3) **what I should not resend until the revised filing is accepted**
4) **who needs to see what, in what order, so I don’t trigger another review round**

Also, if there’s any practical difference between a **corrected document**, **amended filing**, and **re-recording**, spell that out. That’s the part people always blur together and it wastes time.

I’m not looking for theory. I want the clean sequence I can hand to the title officer and the county clerk without getting bounced again.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the tighter operational version.

## 1) First split the county defect into 3 buckets

### A. **Recordability defect**
Examples:
- bad legal description
- wrong acknowledgment / notarization
- missing signatures
- bad margins / font / page size
- missing required cover sheet / transfer form
- fee problem

**Fix first.**  
If it won’t record cleanly, nothing else matters.

---

### B. **Content defect**
Examples:
- wrong vesting name
- wrong parcel ID
- incorrect grantor/grantee
- incorrect property reference
- language conflict inside the document

**Fix before resubmitting to title or county.**  
This is a document problem, not a filing-format problem.

---

### C. **Indexing defect**
Examples:
- clerk indexed the wrong name
- parcel indexed incorrectly
- document recorded but searchable under bad data

**Usually fix after recording** with a correction request, indexing correction, or clerk follow-up.  
This is the lowest priority unless the title company needs the index corrected for their file.

---

## 2) What gets cured first

### If it’s **recordability**
1. fix the document
2. confirm it is county-ready
3. send to title for pre-clearance if needed
4. record
5. then circulate recorded copies

### If it’s **content**
1. fix the substance
2. send revised draft to title first
3. get title to confirm it still matches underwriting / vesting
4. then send to county
5. record
6. then circulate recorded copies

### If it’s **indexing**
1. record the document if the underlying instrument is otherwise valid
2. get stamped copy
3. request indexing correction separately
4. send recorded copy to title

---

## 3) What title will wait on vs. what they’ll clear conditionally

### Title will usually **wait on**:
- corrected deed / transfer instrument
- corrected legal description
- corrected vesting / names
- authority docs if signer authority is in question
- releases or payoffs if they affect insurability
- anything that changes the insured transaction

### Title may **clear conditionally** on:
- county indexing correction
- minor clerical fixes that do not change substance
- post-recording delivery of stamped copies
- follow-up affidavit if they already have the core cure in hand

### Title will **not** usually clear conditionally on:
- unresolved signature authority
- unresolved legal description errors
- unrecordable acknowledgment issues
- missing release that clouds title
- anything that changes who owns what

If it affects insurability, assume title wants it cured before they give a clean go-ahead.

---

## 4) What you should **not resend** until the revised filing is accepted

Do **not** resend the full package if the county version is still unstable.

Hold back:
- final closing package
- title re-review package
- payoff/release distribution
- recording batch to multiple parties
- “final” executed copies

Why: if you circulate the wrong version, people start marking up the wrong draft and you get a second correction cycle.

What you **can** send early:
- the defect list
- the revised draft marked “for review only”
- the one-page summary of what changed
- the specific county comments and your response to each

---

## 5) Clean sequence to avoid another review round

### Step 1 — Get the rejection in writing
From both:
- title company
- county clerk / recorder

### Step 2 — Classify the defect
Mark each item as:
- recordability
- content
- indexing

### Step 3 — Fix the county-facing document first
Do not send a half-fixed version.

### Step 4 — Send revised draft to title for pre-clearance
Ask for one answer:
- “Will this cure the objection?”

Not a general review.

### Step 5 — Once title confirms, send to county
Only send the version title has already seen if the change affects ownership, vesting, or insurability.

### Step 6 — Record or re-record
Then circulate the stamped copy.

### Step 7 — Handle indexing or clerical cleanup separately
Do not hold the whole file open for an indexing issue unless title says they need it.

---

## 6) Corrected document vs. amended filing vs. re-recording

### **Corrected document**
A revised version of the original document that fixes the error before it is recorded.

Use this when:
- the original has not been recorded yet
- the county rejected it
- the substance or formatting needs to be fixed

This is the cleanest fix.

---

### **Amended filing**
A later filing that changes or supplements something already filed.

Use this when:
- the original was already recorded
- you need to modify the record
- the county or title wants an additional instrument, not a replacement

This is common when the original record can’t simply be pulled back.

---

### **Re-recording**
Submitting the document again for recording, usually because the first version was rejected, defective, or needs a corrected record.

Use this when:
- the first submission was not accepted
- the recording office requires a clean replacement
- the corrected document needs to appear in the public record

This is usually what you want after a rejection.

---

## 7) Practical rule of thumb

- **Rejected before recording** → correct the document and re-submit
- **Recorded but wrong** → amend or re-record, depending on the clerk’s process
- **Indexed wrong** → correct indexing separately
- **Title issue affecting insurability** → title cures first, then county

---

## 8) Who sees what, and in what order

### Order:
1. **You / closing coordinator**
2. **Title officer**
3. **Attorney or preparer if needed**
4. **County clerk / recorder**
5. **Title officer again with stamped copy**
6. **Buyer/seller only after final form is accepted**

### What each gets:
- **Title officer:** defect list + revised draft + authority docs + cure items
- **County:** only the final recordable version
- **Buyer/seller:** only the version ready for execution or final confirmation
- **Attorney/preparer:** only if they need to fix substance or authority

Do not send the county an unresolved draft just to “see if it works.” That usually creates a new rejection trail.

---

## 9) The clean handoff language

To title:
> Please confirm whether this revised version cures the rejection and is acceptable for insurability. If anything else is required, send the minimum cure list only.

To county:
> Please record the attached revised version. It has been corrected to address the prior rejection comments.

To everyone else:
> Hold on the prior version. Use this revised draft only.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page decision tree** with:
- **if/then branches**
- **who gets notified**
- **what gets held**
- **what gets reissued**
so you can use it as a live closing control sheet.

user_simulator.text:
Better, but still a little too loose for how I’d run it.

I want the actual **if/then flow** — like:

- if county says **recordability** defect, do X first, and hold Y
- if county says **content** defect, do X first, and do **not** send Y until Z
- if county says **indexing** only, proceed with X and clean up Y separately
- if title says **insurability issue**, stop and cure before county resubmission
- if title says **clerical/conditional**, you can proceed but only with the revised draft they already cleared

And I want the handoff order in a way I can actually use:
1. title gets the defect list
2. revised draft goes to title
3. county only gets the final corrected version
4. recorded copy goes back to title
5. everyone else stays out until that point

Also, your corrected/amended/re-recording split is fine, but I need one more layer: **which one is the safest default when the original was rejected but not recorded**. That’s the scenario I’m dealing with.

So: tighten it into a decision tree, not just headings. I need something I can forward without explaining it twice.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **operational decision tree** version, with the default sequence for a **rejected but unrecorded** original.

---

# Title / County Paperwork Decision Tree

## Starting rule
If the original was **rejected and not recorded**, the **safest default** is:

**corrected document → title pre-clearance if needed → county resubmission → recorded copy back to title**

Not amended filing first, and not re-recording of the bad version.

---

## 1) If the county defect is **recordability**
Examples:
- bad acknowledgment / notarization
- missing signature
- margin / font / page-size issue
- missing cover sheet / transfer form
- fee problem
- legal description not in recordable form

### Do this first:
1. **Fix the document so it is recordable**
2. **Do not send the old version back**
3. If the change affects substance, **send the revised draft to title first**
4. Once title confirms it is acceptable, **send the final corrected version to the county**
5. After recording, **send stamped copy back to title**

### Hold back:
- final closing package
- buyer/seller “final” copies
- any prior draft that failed recording

### Title posture:
- Title will usually **wait** on this.
- They may not clear until the recordable defect is cured.

---

## 2) If the county defect is **content**
Examples:
- wrong vesting name
- wrong grantor/grantee
- wrong parcel ID
- incorrect legal description
- internal language conflict
- document says something different from the deal

### Do this first:
1. **Correct the substance**
2. **Send revised draft to title before county**
3. Get title to confirm the revised version still fits underwriting / insurability
4. Then send the **final corrected version** to county
5. Record
6. Send recorded copy back to title

### Do not send until title clears:
- county resubmission
- final execution package
- any “clean” copy to parties

### Title posture:
- Title will usually **wait** on this.
- If the content changes ownership, vesting, or insured risk, they want it cured first.

---

## 3) If the county defect is **indexing only**
Examples:
- recorded under wrong name
- parcel indexed incorrectly
- searchable data is wrong, but the recorded instrument itself is valid

### Do this first:
1. **Proceed with the valid recorded instrument if recording is otherwise proper**
2. Get the stamped copy
3. Request **indexing correction** separately with the county
4. Send recorded copy to title

### Hold back:
- only the indexing cleanup piece, not the whole deal
- do not reopen the document unless the underlying instrument is also wrong

### Title posture:
- Title will often **clear conditionally** on this.
- They may accept the recorded document and wait on the indexing fix as a clerical follow-up.

---

## 4) If title says it is an **insurability issue**
Examples:
- unresolved lien
- unresolved authority issue
- missing release
- deed / vesting problem
- anything that changes who owns what or what title can insure

### Do this first:
1. **Stop**
2. Cure the title issue before county resubmission
3. Do not send the county a version title has not cleared if the issue affects insurability
4. Only after title confirms the cure, send the corrected filing to county

### Hold back:
- county resubmission
- final execution package
- distribution to buyer/seller as final

### Rule:
If it affects **insurability**, title gets first shot.

---

## 5) If title says it is a **clerical / conditional issue**
Examples:
- minor typo
- formatting fix
- follow-up affidavit
- something title will accept once the revised draft is in hand

### Do this first:
1. Make the revision
2. Send the **revised draft** to title
3. If title says it is conditionally acceptable, proceed with county
4. Record
5. Send the stamped copy back to title

### Do not do:
- resend the full old package
- circulate multiple versions
- send county a draft title has not already cleared

### Rule:
You can proceed, but only on the **revised draft they already cleared conditionally**.

---

# Clean handoff order

Use this exact sequence:

1. **Title gets the defect list**
2. **Revised draft goes to title**
3. **County only gets the final corrected version**
4. **Recorded copy goes back to title**
5. **Everyone else stays out until that point**

That is the cleanest way to avoid another review round.

---

# What not to resend until revised filing is accepted

Do **not** resend:
- the original rejected version
- the full closing package
- final execution copies
- payoff/release stack to everyone
- any document set marked “final” before title has cleared the revision

What you **can** send:
- defect list
- marked revised draft
- county response summary
- title cure list
- one clean version for review

---

# Corrected document vs. amended filing vs. re-recording

## Corrected document
A fixed version of the original document.

Use this when:
- the original was **rejected and not recorded**
- you need to cure the defect before any valid filing

**Safest default in your situation.**

---

## Amended filing
A later filing that changes or supplements something already recorded.

Use this when:
- the original was already recorded
- you need to modify the public record with a new instrument

---

## Re-recording
Submitting the document again for recording, usually after rejection or defect correction.

Use this when:
- the first submission was rejected
- the county wants the same instrument resubmitted in corrected form
- you need the corrected version to appear in the record

---

# Safest default for your scenario
Since the original was **rejected but not recorded**, the safest default is:

**corrected document first**  
then **title pre-clearance if the change affects insurability or substance**  
then **county resubmission**  
then **recorded copy back to title**

Not amended filing unless something has already been recorded.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page if/then chart** with boxes like:

- **county defect type**
- **title response**
- **what to hold**
- **what to send**
- **next action**

so you can forward it as-is.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. This is the useful version. The default on a rejected-but-unrecorded filing is the part I needed nailed down — corrected doc first, not some half-baked amended thing.

The if/then split is clean enough to forward. Especially:
- recordability = fix first, hold the rest
- content = title sees the revision before county does
- indexing only = don’t blow up the whole file over it
- insurability issue = title first, county second

And the handoff order is right. Title first, county only the final corrected version, stamped copy back to title. Everything else stays out until the record is clean.

That’s the sequence. Good.
